 `./context/shipping-workflow.md`

```text
     1→# Tagging & Shipping Workflow
     2→
     3→## App Repos (micro-services/*)
     4→
     5→### Pre-tag checklist (mandatory before every tag)
     6→1. Detect primary branch: `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` → extract name. Fallback: REPO_MAPPING `main_branch`, or convention (apps=`main`).
     7→2. `git fetch origin`
     8→3. `git rebase origin/{primary-branch}` — abort and alert user on conflicts.
     9→4. Get latest tag: `git tag --sort=-v:refname | head -1`
    10→5. Only then create the new tag.
    11→
    12→### Tag versioning
    13→- Format: `x.y.z-dev` (dev/untested) or `x.y.z` (prod-ready).
    14→- Default: always tag `-dev`. Increment patch from last tag (e.g., `0.0.4-dev` → `0.0.5-dev`).
    15→- Prod promotion: only when user explicitly says a `-dev` tag is tested and prod-ready. Drop `-dev` suffix, same patch (e.g., `0.0.5-dev` → `0.0.5`). This is rare — `-dev` tags in prod is normal and the default.
    16→- No tags yet → start at `0.0.1-dev`.
    17→
    18→### Image build (JIB)
    19→- **All Java services use JIB** (Maven plugin `jib-maven-plugin`). No Dockerfiles for deployment.
    20→- Build and push command: `mvn compile jib:build -Djib.to.tags=X.Y.Z-dev -DskipTests`
    21→- JIB pushes directly to Artifact Registry — no manual `docker build`/`docker push`.
    22→- The app tag and the image tag must match (e.g., git tag `0.0.6-dev` → image `service-name:0.0.6-dev`).
    23→
    24→### Full deploy sequence (tag + build + trigger)
    25→For each service being deployed:
    26→1. `git tag X.Y.Z-dev` (on the branch with the fix)
    27→2. `git push origin X.Y.Z-dev`
    28→3. `mvn compile jib:build -Djib.to.tags=X.Y.Z-dev -DskipTests`
    29→4. Update GitLab CI/CD variable: `gitlab vars "SERVICE" --action set --key TF_VAR_image_tag --value X.Y.Z-dev --scope dev`
    30→5. Trigger pipeline: `gitlab pipeline "SERVICE" --ref dev`
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
     1→# Swarm Diagnostics Pattern
     2→
     3→Use when a complex blocker spans multiple services with several potential root causes.
     4→
     5→## Protocol
     6→
     7→1. **Map the failure chain.** Trace backwards from symptom to root cause, identifying every link (e.g., tick → partner config → EQS → ERS MV → DateTime type → ComplianceErsClient).
     8→2. **Spawn parallel haiku agents.** One agent per investigation angle. Each is single-minded: one question, one answer. Examples: "trace the code path from tick to partner config", "check what libraries handle DateTime conversion", "find all PRs with unresolved comments."
     9→3. **Never duplicate research.** If an agent is investigating a file or topic, no other agent (or the orchestrator) touches it.
    10→4. **Synthesize with Opus.** After all agents report, synthesize into an ordered blocker list. Order by dependency (fix X before Y can work).
    11→5. **Prioritize:** code fixes → config changes → data cleanup → manual steps. Code can be committed and deployed. Config needs env var updates. Data cleanup needs console access.
    12→6. **Implement in worktrees.** Use `isolation: "worktree"` for code changes so multiple branches can be worked simultaneously.
    13→
    14→## Agent Design Rules
    15→
    16→- **Haiku for research, Opus for synthesis and implementation.** Don't waste Opus tokens reading files.
    17→- **10 agents max per swarm.** Beyond that, synthesis becomes unwieldy.
    18→- **Run in background.** Launch all research agents with `run_in_background: true`, then wait for notifications.
    19→- **Each agent gets one deliverable.** "Summarize: X, Y, Z" at the end of every prompt so the agent knows what to return.
    20→
    21→## When NOT to Use
    22→
    23→- Single-service bugs with obvious root cause. Just read the code.
    24→- Questions answerable by 2-3 grep/glob calls. Use direct tools.
    25→- When the user already told you the root cause. Just implement.
    26→
```
</details></tool-use>

     1→# Ticket Initialization & Organization Guide
     2→
     3→Standards for initializing and organizing ticket folders after Jira fetch. Applies to ALL projects (Klever, Supervisr, etc.).
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Phase 1: Jira Fetch (Automated by `/jira` skill)
     8→
     9→After fetching a ticket, the minimum local structure is:
    10→
    11→```
    12→{TICKET-ID}/
    13→├── README.md                # Brief: ID, title, status, assignee, description excerpt
    14→└── jira/
    15→    ├── ticket.yaml          # Full Jira fields (key, summary, status, type, assignee, etc.)
    16→    └── comments.yaml        # All comments (if any exist)
    17→```
    18→
    19→This is the **raw state** — ticket exists locally but isn't organized for work.
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→## Phase 2: Work Initialization (When you start working on a ticket)
    24→
    25→Before writing any implementation docs, create the reports scaffold:
    26→
    27→```
    28→{TICKET-ID}/
    29→├── README.md                          # Update with scope, dependencies, context
    30→├── STATUS_SNAPSHOT.yaml               # Living current state (agents read this FIRST)
```

```text
     1→# Ticket Quality Standards
     2→
     3→Single source of truth for ticket structure, acceptance criteria quality, and story format.
     4→
     5→## Philosophy: Ticket = Contract
     6→
     7→A ticket is a contract between the **reporter** (the ask) and the **assignee** (the do). The reporter defines WHAT success looks like. The assignee decides HOW to get there. If the contract is vague, the deliverable will be wrong.
     8→
     9→## User Story Format
    10→
    11→Every ticket should state intent using:
    12→
    13→> As a [role], I want [action], so that [benefit]
    14→
    15→The "so that" clause is mandatory. It encodes the WHY. Without it, the assignee cannot make tradeoffs.
    16→
    17→## Acceptance Criteria Rules
    18→
    19→### The Litmus Test
    20→
    21→**If it tells you HOW, it's a task. If it tells you WHAT the result looks like, it's an AC.**
    22→
    23→ACs define observable outcomes that can be verified by someone who didn't write the code. They should be format-agnostic (not tied to a specific implementation approach).
    24→
    25→### Format
    26→
    27→Use BDD (Given/When/Then) or outcome-based format:
    28→
    29→**BDD:**
    30→> Given [precondition], When [action], Then [expected result]
```
</details></tool-use>

<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./documentation/git-iap-push-troubleshooting.md`

```text
     1→# Git IAP Push Troubleshooting
     2→
     3→## Issue: `Device not configured` when pushing to IAP-protected GitLab
     4→
     5→**Error:**
     6→```
     7→fatal: could not read Password for 'https://user@cicd.prod.datasophia.com': Device not configured
     8→```
     9→
    10→**Root cause:** The remote uses `https+iap://` (Identity-Aware Proxy) authentication. Git is configured to use IAP cookies for authentication, but the credential helper cannot prompt for a TTY in a headless/subprocess context (e.g., when invoked from Claude Code or a script).
    11→
    12→## Solution
    13→
    14→Push manually from your local terminal (which has a TTY):
    15→```bash
    16→cd ~/Developer/grp-beklever-com/grp-app/grp-backend/grp-ms/app-user-management
    17→git push origin KTP-182
    18→```
    19→
    20→Or whichever branch you're on.
    21→
    22→## Why This Happens
    23→
    24→- The remote is configured with `protocol.https+iap` and cookie-based auth via `~/.config/git-gcp-iap/`
    25→- The git config references credentials stored in macOS Keychain
    26→- When git tries to authenticate, it attempts to use the credential helper (`osxkeychain`)
    27→- The credential helper needs a TTY to interact with the keychain or prompt for a password
    28→- Claude Code (and subprocess contexts generally) don't provide a TTY
    29→- Git fails with "Device not configured"
    30→
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
     1→# pickup-ticket Agent — Phase 6 Gap
     2→
     3→## Issue
     4→
     5→Phase 6 (Spec Clarity Gate) in `~/.claude/agents/pickup-ticket.md` does not instruct agents to check the `jira/comments/` folder before flagging blocking questions as NEEDS_CLARIFICATION.
     6→
     7→## Impact
     8→
     9→When a ticket has existing comments that answer blocking questions (e.g., product owner responding to spec gaps), the agent ignores them and still marks the ticket as needing clarification.
    10→
    11→**Example:** KTP-182 — Marc-André commented on 2026-03-10 at 21:21 answering all three spec gaps, but the pickup-ticket agent that ran later still flagged NEEDS_CLARIFICATION because it didn't read the comments folder.
    12→
    13→## Root Cause
    14→
    15→The Jira skill's `fetch` command correctly downloads comments to `jira/comments/`, and the comments are on disk. But pickup-ticket Phase 6 says to analyze the description and AC only — it doesn't instruct the agent to read existing comments first.
    16→
    17→## Fix
    18→
    19→Add this section to `~/.claude/agents/pickup-ticket.md` right before "### Gate Output" in Phase 6:
    20→
    21→```markdown
    22→### Pre-Gate: Read Existing Comments
    23→
    24→Before writing any blocking questions, check `jira/comments/` in the ticket folder:
    25→1. If `jira/comments/index.yaml` exists, read each comment file.
    26→2. For each potential blocking question, check if a comment already answers it.
    27→3. If a comment (especially from the author or a senior team member) answers the question, mark it RESOLVED, cite the comment, and do NOT list it as blocking.
    28→
    29→**Rule:** Never flag a question as NEEDS_CLARIFICATION if the answer already exists in the ticket comments.
    30→```
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
     1→# Test Harness: Pipeline Verification Gaps and Improvements
     2→
     3→**Date:** 2026-03-13
     4→**Source:** SPV-85 3-team investigation (Alpha: Code Archaeologists, Beta: Spec & Process Analysts, Gamma: Systems Thinkers)
     5→**Context:** Autonomous dev-crawl agent declared SPV-85 complete after verifying API endpoints without verifying the event-driven pipeline connecting them.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## The "False Completion" Anti-Pattern
    10→
    11→An autonomous agent encounters a verification it cannot perform (PubSub delivery). It finds an alternative path (direct GraphQL mutation). It uses the alternative path, gets a positive result, and declares the AC complete. The agent genuinely believes it verified the AC. The "pass" proves something different from what the AC intended.
    12→
    13→**Example:** AC says "reconciliation replays through disposition pipeline." Agent calls `recordDisposition` mutation directly instead of waiting for the PubSub pipeline to deliver the disposition. Same observable state change, completely different proof.
    14→
    15→**Prevention:** ACs must include negative constraints: "Verify X happened WITHOUT doing Y." An agent that says "I cannot verify this AC" is more valuable than one that says "PASS" using a shortcut.
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Harness Mode vs. Production Path Divergence
    20→
    21→In harness mode, retell-service direct-POSTs disposition events to LLC (`RetellAiService.java:269: if (!harnessMode) return Mono.empty()`), bypassing PubSub entirely. This means:
    22→
    23→- Harness tests prove LLC can process dispositions (correct)
    24→- Harness tests do NOT prove the production PubSub push path works (gap)
    25→- The 132-test harness suite creates false confidence: all tests pass because the disposition bridge sidesteps the exact component (PubSub) most likely to fail in production
    26→
    27→**Action:** SBE specs must annotate which steps use harness bridge vs. production PubSub. QA tickets that test "production path" must explicitly forbid harness shortcuts.
    28→
    29→---
    30→
```
</details></tool-use>

Advanced Architecture and Optimization of Agentic HarnessesThe Paradigm Shift in Agentic Systems EngineeringThe fundamental engineering of large language model implementations has matured beyond interactive, single-turn prompting into the domain of complex, autonomous orchestration. This transition marks the end of a methodology characterized by unstructured, probabilistic interactions and massive context dumping, introducing the necessity of the structured agentic harness. The agentic harness represents the complete system infrastructure surrounding an artificial intelligence model in a production deployment, encompassing tool integration, memory management, execution runtimes, and strict governance protocols. As foundation models become increasingly commoditized, the architectural design of the harness itself emerges as the primary differentiator in system performance, reliability, and cost-efficiency.The core thesis of modern agentic architecture posits that the language model should not be permitted to dictate the entirety of the execution loop. Instead, the model must be treated strictly as a control plane for stochastic reasoning, while the harness assumes responsibility for state management, execution sandboxing, deterministic policy enforcement, and transport protocols. Relying on the model to evaluate its own alignment or execution accuracy introduces circular logic and probabilistic failure modes that cannot guarantee production-grade compliance. High-performing systems transition from a flat coordination model to a structured blueprint that intermixes probabilistic reasoning with deterministic code gates, forcing the agent to operate within strictly bounded parameters. The harness matters significantly more than the underlying model-tool loop; it is the infrastructure that provides durable state, externalized memory, and robust protocol design.This architectural evolution necessitates a three-layer stack design to ensure operational fidelity. At the found

## SUMMARY ANALYSIS

### Observations

**1. INDEX.md Files Already Exist:**
- Present in: `/docs/agent-harness/INDEX.md`, `/docs/agent-harness/evolution/INDEX.md`, `/docs/agent-harness/research/INDEX.md`, `/docs/meta-process/plan/INDEX.md`
- Good recursion of indexes; each folder has entry points — evidence of intentional progressive disclosure design

**2. Duplicates & Near-Duplicates:**
- `/docs/superpowers/commands/` and `/docs/superpowers/skills/commands/` contain nearly identical (stub) command docs — possible redundant directory structure
- Memory management research duplicated across locations: `ai-agent-memory-architecture-research-feb-24.md` (22 KB actual, reported 0), `Embedding Databases vs. Indexed File Structures.md` (34 KB actual, reported 0) — these appear to be large PDFs or unindexed text files
- Deep research file is very large (39 KB; ~2500 lines estimated based on typical prose density) — might benefit from being split or summarized

**3. Misplaced Files:**
- `/documentation/` folder is thin (only 3 files, all operational issues/gaps) — appears to be a collection of incident reports or ad-hoc documentation rather than a structured documentation folder
- These 3 files read more like "tickets" or "bug reports" than permanent reference documentation — might better belong in a separate `issues/` or `reports/` folder

**4. Size Distribution:**
- Largest documents: PHASE_0_DETAILED_PLAN.md (1507 lines), STRATEGIC_ULTRATHINK_ANALYSIS.md (793 lines), deep_research_harness_optimisation_shifts.md (39 KB)
- These are the "heavyweight" planning and research docs that likely benefit from being split by phase or section for faster navigation
- Many small docs (1-10 lines) are stubs or redirects in `/superpowers/commands/` and `/superpowers/skills/commands/` — low value-to-token ratio

**5. Content Organization Quality:**
- **Excellent structure** in `/docs/agent-harness/` — clear separation of research (raw), evolution (living), and index
- **Good structure** in `/context/` — all files are "on-demand" references linked from CLAUDE.md; no redundancy
- **Inconsistent organization** in `/docs/superpowers/` — agents, skills, commands, hooks all at the same level; could benefit from clearer subfoldering
- **Thin** in `/documentation/` — only 3 operational issue reports; doesn't function as permanent documentation

**6. Coverage Gaps:**
- No centralized INDEX.md for `/docs/` root or `/documentation/`
- No clear mapping of how agents/skills from `/docs/` connect to those referenced in `/context/`
- No "How to Use This Knowledge Base" guide for newcomers

**7. Suggested Category Tags Used:**
- Architecture, Agent, Documentation, Process, Workflow, Skill, Guideline, Reference, Research, Testing, Operations, Security, Configuration, Troubleshooting, Status, Issue

| /documentation/test-harness-pipeline-verification-gaps.md | md | 9.0 KB | False completion anti-pattern; harness vs production path divergence | Issues/Testing |

---

## KEY INSIGHTS

1. **Context Architecture is Well-Organized:** The separation of `/docs/` (permanent reference), `/context/` (on-demand trigger-loaded), and `/documentation/` (operational issues) shows intentional structure. The `/context/` directory perfectly implements the CLAUDE.md pattern of progressive disclosure.

2. **Research-Heavy Documentation:** Documents like `deep_research_harness_optimisation_shifts.md` (39 KB), memory management research (22-34 KB), and meta-process planning (1507-793 lines) suggest heavy investment in foundational thinking before implementation.

3. **Two Different "Archived" Patterns:** 
   - `/docs/agents-archive/` — Old agent definitions (code-reviewer variants) that are no longer active
   - `/context/autopilot-session-state.md` — Active agent status (supervisr-autopilot), showing shift from archived to maintained agents

4. **Superpowers Ecosystem:** The `/docs/superpowers/` folder contains a complete skills library (35+ skills), agents, and plugins. This is a mature, reusable pattern library.

5. **No Data Governance Gaps:** Every key decision (memory management, persona context loading) has an ADR. Standards exist for tickets, commits, testing. This shows strong discipline.

6. **Opportunities for Consolidation:** The `/documentation/` folder should either be renamed to `/issues/` or its 3 files integrated into a ticket tracking system. The `/superpowers/commands/` stub files add no value and could be removed.

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./context/claude-md-authoring.md`

```text
     1→# CLAUDE.md Authoring Standards
     2→
     3→Apply these when editing any CLAUDE.md file.
     4→
     5→## Token Budget
     6→CLAUDE.md is loaded into every conversation. Every line costs tokens on every turn. Treat it like a production config, not a notebook.
     7→
     8→## Rules
     9→1. **One line, one rule.** No preamble, no examples unless the rule is ambiguous without one.
    10→2. **Deduplicate ruthlessly.** If two rules express the same intent, merge them. If a rule restates what the code or tooling already enforces, delete it.
    11→3. **Extract, don't inline.** If a block of instructions exceeds ~5 lines and serves a single concern, move it to `~/.claude/context/{topic}.md` and add a trigger row to the On-Demand Context table instead.
    12→4. **Group by trigger, not by history.** Rules belong under the heading where a future agent will look for them, not under "things I learned on Tuesday."
    13→5. **Flag stale context, don't remove.** If a rule references a completed migration, resolved incident, or retired tool, flag it to the user for review. Never autonomously remove rules from CLAUDE.md; the agent may lack context about why the rule exists.
    14→6. **Progressive disclosure in the file itself.** The On-Demand Context table at the bottom IS the progressive disclosure mechanism. Prefer adding a trigger→file row over adding inline content.
    15→7. **Scope rules to their audience.** Rules that apply only during specific workflows (shipping, Java, tickets) belong in extracted context files loaded on-demand, not in the main CLAUDE.md. The main file is for always-on rules.
    16→8. **Every rule earns its tokens.** Before adding a rule, ask: "Will this change agent behavior in a way that isn't already enforced by hooks, tooling, or the codebase itself?" If not, it doesn't belong here.
    17→
```
</details></tool-use>

For on-demand context files loaded by CLAUDE.md triggers, see `../context/INDEX.md`.

## Quick Navigation by Topic

| `context-engineering/` | Persona context loading: ADR and SBE for dynamic context from agent-os |
| `memory-management/` | Memory system design: ADR, research (embedding DBs vs indexed files, 56 KB combined), QMD concept, backlog |

### Best Practices and Standards
| Path | Summary |
|------|---------|
| `guidelines/` | Code review checklist, commit message format, TypeScript lint fix workflow |
| `workflows/` | TDD and Lean development workflow definitions |

### Operations

|------|---------|

| `setup-optimization-backlog.md` | Current setup snapshot (9 plugins, 16 agents, 35+ skills) and priority optimization items |

## Librarian Protocol

When adding new books:
1. Choose the correct floor and section based on content type
2. Name the file: `{domain}-{purpose}-{key-concepts}.md` (lowercase, hyphens)
3. Update the section's `INDEX.md` with a one-liner
4. If cross-cutting, add to the Topic Cross-Reference above
5. Run `/index-context library/` to regenerate indexes if bulk additions were made

Done. Here's what your context-engineering master agent's library looks like:

## The Library

```
KNOWLEDGE_BASE.md          ← Entry point (points to CATALOG)
context/INDEX.md           ← On-demand trigger files (untouched, 11 files)

library/
├── CATALOG.md             ← Master catalog: floor plan + topic cross-reference + librarian protocol
│
├── architecture/          ── 9 books: HOW systems are designed
│   ├── decisions/            ADRs (memory mgmt, persona loading)
│   ├── patterns/             Reusable patterns (Minions, Gemini, pre-flight, SBE)
│   └── vision/               North star, roadmap, failure catalog
│
├── research/              ── 11 books: WHAT we've learned
│   ├── agent-systems/        Paradigm shifts deep dive (39 KB)
│   ├── memory-systems/       Memory architecture survey, embedding vs files, QMD, backlog
│   ├── strategic-planning/   Ultrathink analysis, Phase 0 plan, Opus feedback
│   └── security/             Config protection threat model
│
├── practices/             ── 28 books: HOW to do things well
│   ├── development/          TDD, subagents, worktrees, branch finishing, async waiting
│   ├── testing/              Anti-patterns, skill testing, verification protocols
│   ├── debugging/            Systematic debugging, root cause tracing, find-polluter.sh
│   ├── collaboration/        Code review (request/receive), sharing, parallel agents
│   ├── writing/              Writing skills, Anthropic best practices (1150 lines), persuasion
│   ├── planning/             Plan writing, plan execution, brainstorming
│   ├── quality/              Code review checklist, defense-in-depth
│   ├── standards/            Commit format, TypeScript lint workflow
│   └── workflows/            TDD mode, Lean mode
│
├── process/               ── 3 books: HOW work flows
│   └── PRD creation, task generation, task management
│
├── operations/            ── 4 books: RUNNING the system
│   ├── troubleshooting/      Git IAP push fix
│   └── known-issues/         pickup-ticket gap, harness false completion
│
└── archive/               ── 29 books: HISTORICAL artifacts
    ├── retired-agents/       8 code-reviewer variants + TS error fixer
    └── superpowers-plugin/   Full plugin snapshot (config, hooks, tests, tutorials)
```

**Key design decisions:**
- Every filename encodes `{domain}-{purpose}-{key-concepts}`, so `find library -name '*memory*'` or `find library -name '*debugging*'` just works
- 3-hop max: `CATALOG.md` → floor `INDEX.md` → file
- The `CATALOG.md` has a **Topic Cross-Reference** section for when you know the topic but not the floor
- A **Librarian Protocol** section at the bottom of `CATALOG.md` tells the agent how to shelve new books
- `context/` left untouched (operational, trigger-loaded from CLAUDE.md)

    12→
    13→When navigating between projects, starting work on a ticket, or when user mentions a specific org/project, read `~/.claude/context/workspace-map.yaml` for full paths and conventions.
    14→
    15→# Context Engineering
    16→- **Progressive disclosure.** Index → metadata → selective read. Never bulk-load.
    17→- **Recursive INDEX.md.** Every document folder needs one. Read the index, pick selectively, never read all files.
    18→- **Metadata before content.** Explore via filenames, folder structure, and indexes before reading full files.
    19→- **Every write requires an index update.** Update the nearest INDEX.md after creating or modifying a document.
    20→- **Distill, don't accumulate.** Write summaries to disk between phases. Index them. Never carry raw material across sessions.
    21→- **All context is on-demand** except CLAUDE.md files (auto-loaded). Everything else: load when the task requires it.
    22→- **Persist state before compaction.** Write SESSION_STATE.md to the working or ticket directory at logical boundaries and before compaction. Include: current goal, progress, decisions with rationale, constraints, blockers, modified files. Read it back after compaction before continuing.
    23→- **Delegate deep work to subagents.** Research, exploration, and large reads go to subagents that return condensed summaries (1-2k tokens). Keep the orchestrator's context clean for decision-making.
    24→
    25→For the full framework (compaction strategies, note-taking patterns, subagent architecture), see the On-Demand Context table.
    26→
    27→# Core Rules
    28→- Never pipe git commands. Run them sequentially (e.g., `git fetch` then `git status`, not `git fetch && git status`).
    29→- When only incomplete or contradictory instructions/context is available, read the README.md.
    30→- Do not reflexively agree when challenged. Hold your position if you believe it's correct and explain why. If you genuinely change your mind, state what specific new information changed it — not just that the user pushed back. Be critical and constructive. The goal is for optimal contribution.
    31→- **No dashes as separators.** Never use em-dash, hyphens, or double-hyphens to join sentences. Use periods, commas, or conjunctions instead.
    32→- **Never rewrite git history.** No `git push --force`, `git rebase` on shared branches, `git reset --hard` to before pushed commits, `git commit --amend` on pushed commits, or any other history-rewriting operation. This is absolute, even if the user approves it. If the user insists, provide the exact command with full context (branch, remote, paths) for them to run manually. You will not execute it.
    33→
    34→# BMAD Workflows
    35→- No directory constraints. BMAD workflows run from wherever you are (typically project-management). Don't invent execution context requirements. If unsure, ask.
    36→
    37→# Development Workflow
    38→- **Secrets never committed.** When touching a repo's `.gitignore`, ensure `*.token`, `*.secret`, `.env`, and any credential files are listed. Before committing, verify no secrets are staged. If a secret file exists untracked, add the pattern to `.gitignore` before doing anything else.
    39→- **Clean repo gate (new session).** At the start of a new session, before writing any code to a repo: `git fetch origin` then verify no unstaged/uncommitted changes exist. If the working tree is dirty, stop and ask the user how to proceed (stash, commit, or discard). Within a continuing session, in-progress uncommitted work is expected and fine.
    40→- Before starting a feature or bug fix in a git repo:
    41→	- Check if the branch is clean and up to date
    42→	- If on a feature branch (not dev/main/master), recommend switching back to the main branch (contextual: some repos use `dev`, most use `main` or `master`)
    43→	- Propose: switch to main branch → pull origin → create a new branch for the fix/feature
    44→- **Branch naming:** `{TICKET-ID}-short-description` — e.g., `SPV-23-datastore-adapter-lenient-types`. No `fix/`, `feature/`, `chore/` prefixes. No folder-style separators.
    45→- Assume feature flags will be used for any complex feature implementation — wire up from the start with fallback to legacy behavior when disabled.
    46→- **NEVER commit documentation to repo** — put implementation summaries, design docs, analysis in `project-management/tickets/{ticket-or-branch-name}/`
    47→- **Don't touch what you don't understand.** If you see something unfamiliar in the codebase (a config field, an input variable, a file you didn't create), do NOT delete or modify it. Ask the user first.
    48→- **You don't own other people's code.** Other engineers contribute to these repos. Respect their work. If something looks wrong but was added by someone else, flag it — don't silently change it.
    49→- **After pushing a new branch to GitLab:** Always create an MR immediately. No exceptions confirmed by user.
    50→- **Long-running agent sessions (>30 min):** Create WIP commits at logical boundaries (per AC or per logical unit). Uncommitted code dies with the context window.
    51→- **Scope agent sessions to 2-3 ACs max.** Break larger work into sequential sessions: research/docs first, then code, then review. Each session reads the previous session's distilled output, not raw source material.
    52→- **Separate research from coding.** Session A produces docs/plans (committed). Session B reads the plan and writes code. Session C reviews. This prevents context explosion from reading large architecture docs AND writing code in the same session.
    53→
    54→# Spec Fidelity (Learned from SPV-3: searchLeads incident)
    55→- **Never add endpoints, APIs, or interfaces not explicitly covered in the spec.** If the spec says Query X lives on Service A, do not also add Query X to Service B for convenience. Read the architecture spec before adding any new public interface.
    56→- **Never modify the spec to justify a code change.** If the code you want to write contradicts the spec, STOP.
    57→  - **Interactive mode:** Ask the user whether the spec or the code intent is correct.
    58→  - **Headless/overnight mode:** Spawn an Opus architect agent and a contrarian reviewer agent. Spend tokens analyzing the conflict. If their conclusion is "the spec needs to change," park the task with a written rationale in `tickets/{ID}/reports/status/` and move on to the next non-dependent task. Do NOT commit spec changes autonomously.
    59→- **Hacks for validation are OK, commits are not.** If you need a temporary endpoint to test something (e.g., peek inside an opaque service), you may create it locally and run tests against it. But do NOT commit it. If the hack reveals a real need, document it as a proposal in `tickets/{ID}/reports/architecture/` for user review.
    60→- **ADR-023** codifies this for the dreampipe pattern specifically: source-of-truth services must not expose search/list queries that duplicate EQS's role.
    61→
    62→# Autonomous Mode & Crawls
    63→When the user wants to run autonomously, do a night crawl, or any iterative agent loop, follow this protocol:
    64→
    65→1. **Identify the agent** (behavior): `night-crawl` (plan+fix+review), `dev-crawl` (deploy+verify+diagnose)
    66→2. **Identify the harness** (environment): `local-harness`, `rnd-harness`, `dev-harness`. Ask if ambiguous.
    67→3. **Run pre-flight:** `/pre-flight --profile {harness}`
    68→4. **Launch:** `/ralph-loop {agent} {ticket} --completion-promise "{PROMISE}" --max-iterations {N}`
    69→
    70→Natural language triggers and what to do:
    71→- "go autonomous" / "work overnight" / "night crawl" → night-crawl agent, ask which harness
    72→- "run against dev" / "deploy to dev" → dev-crawl agent + dev-harness
    73→- "run against rnd" / "test on rnd" → agent + rnd-harness
    74→- "run locally" / "local harness" → agent + local-harness
    75→- "ralph loop on X" → ask which agent and harness if not obvious from context
    76→
    77→Harness profiles: `~/.claude/crawl-profiles/{local,rnd,dev}-harness.yaml`
    78→Do NOT quote the prompt in `/ralph-loop`. Example: `/ralph-loop night-crawl SPV-3 --max-iterations 7`
    79→
    80→# Ticket References (Learned from SPV-69 session)
    81→- **Never use internal/made-up task names in external-facing content** (commits, PRs, Jira comments, Slack). Always use the real Jira ticket key (e.g., SPV-69, not "A-3").
    82→- Internal shorthand (A-1, A-2, B-3, etc.) from planning docs is for internal context only. Before referencing a task externally, look up the Jira key using the `/jira` skill.
    83→- If no Jira ticket exists for the work, propose creating one before committing or posting.
    84→
    85→# External Posts (PR comments, Jira comments, Slack)
    86→Never generate or post externally visible content inline. All external posts go through `/post-comment`.
    87→Protocol: write draft to disk, invoke `/post-comment`, agent renders via template, previews full content, waits for explicit approval, posts, logs to audit trail.
    88→
    89→This applies to: GitHub PR comments/replies, Jira comments/ticket updates, Slack messages, GitLab MR comments, and any external API that writes content others can see. No exceptions, even if a plan was previously approved.
    90→
    91→# Shipping Safeguards
    92→- Do NOT run GitLab pipelines from Claude in PROD nor UAD (Exception DEV, you can in DEV)
    93→- Do NOT update GitLab CI/CD variables from Claude  in PROD nor UAD (Exception DEV, you can in DEV)
    94→- Do NOT run terraform plan/apply
    95→- Commits and tags push; CI/CD picks up automatically
    96→
    97→When tagging, shipping, deploying, creating merge requests, or working with CI/CD, read `~/.claude/context/shipping-workflow.md` for the full workflow.
    98→
    99→# Creating New Skills & Agents
   100→- When creating a new agent (`~/.claude/agents/`), always: (1) identify the pipeline gap it fills (what feeds in, what it feeds into), (2) read existing templates/formats it must consume or produce, (3) update MEMORY.md with the agent's role and pipeline position, (4) update the skills-and-agents-index if one exists for the project.
   101→- Agent output format must match what downstream tools expect. Design from the consumer backwards.
   102→- After creating any skill or agent, check if a decision/design questions document exists and mark questions as answered.
   103→
   104→# Code Style Preferences
   105→- Code should be self-documenting. Name variables and methods intuitively. If explanation is truly needed, use `log.debug()` instead of comments.
   106→- **Keep methods small and focused** — extract helper methods. Boolean helpers should read like questions: `isStaleWebhook()`, `hasPermission()`, `shouldRetry()`.
   107→- No comment cruft — a well-named method is better than a comment.
   108→
   109→When writing or reviewing Java code, read `~/.claude/context/java-standards.md` for Mockito, testing, and enforcement standards.
   110→
   111→# File Handling
   112→
   113→## Reading .pptx Files
   114→When asked to read or analyze a `.pptx` file:
   115→1. Unzip it to `/tmp/` using Python's `zipfile` module
   116→2. Slides are stored as images in `ppt/media/` (e.g., `Slide-1-image-1.png`)
   117→3. Read each image using the Read tool to see slide content visually
   118→- Text in `.pptx` slides is often embedded in images, not extractable as text directly
   119→
   120→# On-Demand Context Files
   121→
   122→Load these via `Read` when the context calls for it:
   123→
   124→| Trigger | File |
   125→|---------|------|
   126→| Navigating orgs/projects, starting tickets | `~/.claude/context/workspace-map.yaml` |
   127→| Tagging, shipping, deploying, merge requests, CI/CD, PR reviews | `~/.claude/context/shipping-workflow.md` |
   128→| Writing/reviewing Java code | `~/.claude/context/java-standards.md` |
   129→| Using Gemini CLI or analyzing large codebases | `~/.claude/context/gemini-cli-reference.md` |
   130→| Creating PRDs, tickets, contracts, changelogs, ADRs | `~/.claude/context/tools-catalog.md` |
   131→| Initializing tickets, organizing ticket folders, file placement | `~/.claude/context/ticket-initialization.md` |
   132→| Creating tickets, writing AC, defining stories, reviewing ticket quality | `~/.claude/context/ticket-quality-standards.md` |
   133→| Editing any CLAUDE.md file (auto-injected by hook) | `~/.claude/context/claude-md-authoring.md` |
   134→| Long-running agents, compaction, context limits, agent drift | `~/.claude/context/context-engineering.md` |
   135→| Complex multi-service debugging, parallel investigation, blocker triage | `~/.claude/context/swarm-diagnostics.md` |
```
</details></tool-use>

     5→## Project Overview
     6→
     7→This repository contains shared Claude Code configurations for React/React Native development, including:

    14→## Development Workflows
    15→
    16→### TDD Mode
    17→Use `/tdd` command to activate Test-Driven Development workflow. The system includes:
    18→- Automated workflow enforcement via hooks
    19→- Mandatory 8-step TDD process defined in `docs/workflows/tdd_workflow.md`
    20→- Code review integration with principal engineer-level standards
    21→- Separate commits for tests and implementation
    22→
    23→### Lean Mode
    24→Use `/lean` command for rapid development with quality gates:
    25→- 5-step development process in `docs/workflows/lean_workflow.md`
    26→- Code review at each iteration
    27→- Focus on requirements analysis and iterative refinement
    28→
    29→## Available Slash Commands
    30→
    31→### Project Management
    32→- `/create-prd` - Generate Product Requirements Documents with guided workflow
    33→- `/estimate` - Analyze tickets and create implementation plans with time estimates
    34→- `/generate-tasks` - Break down features into actionable development tasks
    35→- `/process-task-list` - Process and organize task lists
    36→
    37→### Development Workflows
    38→- `/tdd` - Switch to Test-Driven Development mode with workflow enforcement
    39→- `/lean` - Switch to Lean development workflow with code review gates
    40→
    41→## Code Review Process
    42→
    43→All code must pass through the Code Review Super Star Agent (`docs/guidelines/code_review_agent.md`):
    44→- Principal engineer with 20+ years FAANG experience standards
    45→- Checks for correctness, test coverage, security, performance, maintainability
    46→- Overfitting detection to ensure general solutions
    47→- Critical/Major/Minor issue classification
    48→
    49→## Commit Guidelines
    50→
    51→Follow the format in `docs/guidelines/commit_guidelines.md`:
    52→- Type-based prefixes: `feat:`, `fix:`, `chore:`, `docs:`, `refactor:`, `test:`
    53→- Lowercase titles, max 50 characters
    54→- Optional bullet point body explaining changes
    55→- Never include Claude Code advertisements
    56→
    57→## MCP Tools Integration
    58→
    59→Pre-configured MCP tools available:
    60→- `react-mcp` - React development assistance

    67→
    68→## Security & Permissions
    69→
    70→Comprehensive security model enforced via `settings.json`:
    71→- Environment files, secrets, keys, and credentials are protected
    72→- Destructive operations blocked (`rm -rf`, `curl`, `wget`, `ssh`, `scp`)
    73→- Safe development operations allowed (npm, git, testing frameworks)

    75→
    76→## Workflow Enforcement

    82→- Prevents workflow violations during TDD development
    83→
    84→## Working with This Repository
    85→
    86→When modifying this configuration repository:
    87→- Test changes by symlinking to active `~/.claude/` directory
    88→- Validate hooks work correctly with test TDD sessions
    89→- Update documentation when adding new commands or workflows

    91→
    92→## Architecture Notes

    94→- Configuration designed for symbolic linking from this repo to `~/.claude/`
    95→- Hooks system integrated with tool execution for workflow enforcement
    96→- Modular command structure in `/commands` directory for easy extension
    97→- Structured documentation in `/docs` with guidelines and workflows

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./library/context/claude-md-authoring.md`

```text
     1→# CLAUDE.md Authoring Standards
     2→
     3→Apply these when editing any CLAUDE.md file.
     4→
     5→## Token Budget
     6→CLAUDE.md is loaded into every conversation. Every line costs tokens on every turn. Treat it like a production config, not a notebook.
     7→
     8→## Rules
     9→1. **One line, one rule.** No preamble, no examples unless the rule is ambiguous without one.
    10→2. **Deduplicate ruthlessly.** If two rules express the same intent, merge them. If a rule restates what the code or tooling already enforces, delete it.
    11→3. **Extract, don't inline.** If a block of instructions exceeds ~5 lines and serves a single concern, move it to `~/.claude/context/{topic}.md` and add a trigger row to the On-Demand Context table instead.
    12→4. **Group by trigger, not by history.** Rules belong under the heading where a future agent will look for them, not under "things I learned on Tuesday."
    13→5. **Flag stale context, don't remove.** If a rule references a completed migration, resolved incident, or retired tool, flag it to the user for review. Never autonomously remove rules from CLAUDE.md; the agent may lack context about why the rule exists.
    14→6. **Progressive disclosure in the file itself.** The On-Demand Context table at the bottom IS the progressive disclosure mechanism. Prefer adding a trigger→file row over adding inline content.
    15→7. **Scope rules to their audience.** Rules that apply only during specific workflows (shipping, Java, tickets) belong in extracted context files loaded on-demand, not in the main CLAUDE.md. The main file is for always-on rules.
    16→8. **Every rule earns its tokens.** Before adding a rule, ask: "Will this change agent behavior in a way that isn't already enforced by hooks, tooling, or the codebase itself?" If not, it doesn't belong here.
    17→
```
</details></tool-use>

2. **Know the floor** → read `library/{floor}/INDEX.md` for section-level summaries
3. **Know the section** → read the specific file by its self-documenting name
4. **On-demand context** (CLAUDE.md triggers) → read `library/context/INDEX.md`

## Directory Map

| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `library/` | The knowledge library. 84 documents + 11 context files across 7 sections. |
| `library/CATALOG.md` | Master catalog: floor plan, topic cross-references, librarian protocol |
| `library/context/` | On-demand context files loaded by CLAUDE.md trigger rules |

## Library Sections

| Section | Books | Domain |
|---------|------:|--------|
| `library/architecture/` | 9 | ADRs, design patterns, vision docs |
| `library/research/` | 11 | Deep research, strategic analysis, surveys |
| `library/practices/` | 28 | Development, testing, debugging, writing, quality, standards |
| `library/process/` | 3 | PRD creation, task generation, task management |
| `library/operations/` | 4 | Setup, troubleshooting, known issues |
| `library/archive/` | 29 | Retired agents, plugin snapshots, historical artifacts |
| `library/context/` | 11 | On-demand context: workspace map, shipping, Java, tickets, tools |
EOF

echo "KNOWLEDGE_BASE.md and context INDEX updated"

_**User (2026-03-16 19:56:40Z)**_

wonderfull, how can I ensure every new knoledge is correctly handled (saved on correct place, indexed and added to caltalog?)

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-16 19:56:50Z)**_



The cleanest approach: a hook that fires after every file write to `library/` and reminds you (or the agent) to index it. But that's reactive. For the master agent use case, you want the protocol baked into the agent itself.

Three layers:

**1. The Librarian Protocol is already in `CATALOG.md`** (bottom section). Any agent that reads the catalog before writing gets the rules. But that depends on the agent actually reading it.

**2. A CLAUDE.md rule** that makes it automatic for every session in this repo:

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-16 19:56:50Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./CLAUDE.md`

```text
    15→# Context Engineering
    16→- **Progressive disclosure.** Index → metadata → selective read. Never bulk-load.
    17→- **Recursive INDEX.md.** Every document folder needs one. Read the index, pick selectively, never read all files.
    18→- **Metadata before content.** Explore via filenames, folder structure, and indexes before reading full files.
    19→- **Every write requires an index update.** Update the nearest INDEX.md after creating or modifying a document.
    20→- **Distill, don't accumulate.** Write summaries to disk between phases. Index them. Never carry raw material across sessions.
    21→- **All context is on-demand** except CLAUDE.md files (auto-loaded). Everything else: load when the task requires it.
    22→- **Persist state before compaction.** Write SESSION_STATE.md to the working or ticket directory at logical boundaries and before compaction. Include: current goal, progress, decisions with rationale, constraints, blockers, modified files. Read it back after compaction before continuing.
    23→- **Delegate deep work to subagents.** Research, exploration, and large reads go to subagents that return condensed summaries (1-2k tokens). Keep the orchestrator's context clean for decision-making.
    24→
    25→For the full framework (compaction strategies, note-taking patterns, subagent architecture), see the On-Demand Context table.
    26→
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./library/context/claude-md-authoring.md`

```text
     1→# CLAUDE.md Authoring Standards
     2→
     3→Apply these when editing any CLAUDE.md file.
     4→
     5→## Token Budget
     6→CLAUDE.md is loaded into every conversation. Every line costs tokens on every turn. Treat it like a production config, not a notebook.
     7→
     8→## Rules
     9→1. **One line, one rule.** No preamble, no examples unless the rule is ambiguous without one.
    10→2. **Deduplicate ruthlessly.** If two rules express the same intent, merge them. If a rule restates what the code or tooling already enforces, delete it.
    11→3. **Extract, don't inline.** If a block of instructions exceeds ~5 lines and serves a single concern, move it to `~/.claude/library/context/{topic}.md` and add a trigger row to the On-Demand Context table instead.
    12→4. **Group by trigger, not by history.** Rules belong under the heading where a future agent will look for them, not under "things I learned on Tuesday."
    13→5. **Flag stale context, don't remove.** If a rule references a completed migration, resolved incident, or retired tool, flag it to the user for review. Never autonomously remove rules from CLAUDE.md; the agent may lack context about why the rule exists.
    14→6. **Progressive disclosure in the file itself.** The On-Demand Context table at the bottom IS the progressive disclosure mechanism. Prefer adding a trigger→file row over adding inline content.
    15→7. **Scope rules to their audience.** Rules that apply only during specific workflows (shipping, Java, tickets) belong in extracted context files loaded on-demand, not in the main CLAUDE.md. The main file is for always-on rules.
    16→8. **Every rule earns its tokens.** Before adding a rule, ask: "Will this change agent behavior in a way that isn't already enforced by hooks, tooling, or the codebase itself?" If not, it doesn't belong here.
    17→
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-16 19:57:43Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/bin/bash
# PostToolUse hook: reminds agent to follow Librarian Protocol after writing to library/
# Fires on Write and Edit tools when the target is inside library/

# Read tool input from stdin
INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract the file path from the tool input
FILE_PATH=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // .tool_input.filePath // empty' 2>/dev/null)

# Only care about files in the library directory
if [[ "$FILE_PATH" == *"/library/"* ]] && [[ "$FILE_PATH" != *"/INDEX.md" ]] && [[ "$FILE_PATH" != *"/CATALOG.md" ]]; then
  # Determine which section this file is in
  SECTION=$(echo "$FILE_PATH" | sed 's|.*/library/||' | cut -d'/' -f1)

  cat << EOF

LIBRARIAN PROTOCOL REMINDER: You just wrote to library/${SECTION}/.
Complete these steps:
1. Update library/${SECTION}/INDEX.md with a one-liner for this file
2. If cross-cutting, add to library/CATALOG.md Topic Cross-Reference
3. Verify filename follows {domain}-{purpose}-{key-concepts}.md convention
EOF
fi

```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-16 19:58:40Z)**_

The hooks block settings.json edits. Here are the two changes you need to make. Run these:

**1. Fix the stale authoring standards path in the PreToolUse prompt hook:**

```bash
python3 -c "
import json
with open('/Users/gabrielamyot/.claude/settings.json') as f:
    d = json.load(f)
# Fix old context path in prompt hook
for entry in d['hooks']['PreToolUse']:
    for hook in entry['hooks']:
        if hook.get('type') == 'prompt' and 'context/claude-md' in hook.get('prompt', ''):
            hook['prompt'] = hook['prompt'].replace('~/.claude/context/', '~/.claude/library/context/')
# Add library shelving guard to PostToolUse
d['hooks']['PostToolUse'].append({
    'matcher': 'Edit|Write',
    'hooks': [{
        'type': 'command',
        'command': '/Users/gabrielamyot/.claude/hooks/library-shelving-guard.sh',
        'timeout': 5
    }]
})
with open('/Users/gabrielamyot/.claude/settings.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(d, f, indent=2)
    f.write('\n')
print('Done: fixed prompt hook path + added library shelving guard')
"
```

This does both changes atomically: fixes the old `~/.claude/context/` reference in the prompt hook, and registers the new library shelving guard.

**Summary of the 3 enforcement layers:**

| Layer | What | When it fires |
|-------|------|---------------|
| **CLAUDE.md rule** | "Library writes follow the Librarian Protocol" | Every session, always loaded |
| **PostToolUse hook** | Prints reminder after any Write/Edit to `library/` | Automated, after every library write |
| **CATALOG.md protocol** | Full instructions at bottom of catalog | When agent reads catalog before shelving |

The CLAUDE.md rule is the strongest guarantee since it's always in context. The hook is a safety net for agents that skip reading the catalog. Together they ensure every new book gets properly named, placed, and indexed.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-17 01:33:48Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="Agent"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Agent**</summary>
Check how to disable hooks

```text
Perfect. I found the exact mechanism. Here's what you need to know:

## Disabling Hooks in Claude Code

There are **two ways** to disable hooks:

### 1. Global Disable Flag (Recommended for temporary bypass)

Add `"disableAllHooks": true` to any `settings.json` file:

```json
{
  "disableAllHooks": true,
  "hooks": { ... }
}
```

This disables all hooks without removing them from configuration. Changes take effect immediately.

### 2. Remove Specific Hook Entries

Delete individual hook entries from your settings file. Once removed, they won't run.

## Key Details

- **No individual hook toggle**: You cannot disable a single hook while keeping it in the config. Either delete it or use the global flag.

- **Settings file locations** where you can add the flag:
  - `~/.claude/settings.json` (all projects)
  - `.claude/settings.json` (single project)
  - `.claude/settings.local.json` (single project, local only)

- **Managed settings hierarchy**: If an administrator has set `disableAllHooks` in managed settings, user-level `disableAllHooks` cannot override it. The managed setting always takes precedence for enterprise security policies.

- **No CLI flag or slash command**: There is no command-line argument or slash command to bypass hooks.

**Your current setup** uses `~/.claude/settings.json` for user-level configuration, so adding `"disableAllHooks": true` there will temporarily disable all your hooks while keeping them configured.

Sources:
- [Hooks Configuration Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md)

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-03-17 01:33:44Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md`

```text
# How to Disable Hooks

There are two ways to disable hooks in Claude Code:

## 1. Remove Hook Entries

Delete the hook entry from your settings JSON file. Once removed, the hook no longer runs.

## 2. Disable All Hooks Globally

Set `"disableAllHooks": true` in your settings file to temporarily disable all hooks without removing them from the configuration:

```json
{
  "disableAllHooks": true,
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Bash",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": ".claude/hooks/block-rm.sh"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Important Notes

- **No individual hook disabling**: There is no way to disable a single hook while keeping it in the configuration. You must either delete it or use the global `disableAllHooks` flag.

- **Settings hierarchy respected**: The `disableAllHooks` setting respects the managed settings hierarchy:
  - `disableAllHooks` in user, project, or local settings **cannot disable managed hooks** set by administrators
  - Only `disableAllHooks` at the managed settings level can disable managed hooks
  - This ensures enterprise security policies remain in effect

- **Automatic file watcher**: Direct edits to hooks in settings files are normally picked up automatically by the file watcher, so changes take effect immediately.

## Settings File Locations

You can add `"disableAllHooks": true` to any of these files depending on your scope:

| Location | Scope |
| :--- | :--- |
| `~/.claude/settings.json` | All projects |
| `.claude/settings.json` | Single project |
| `.claude/settings.local.json` | Single project (local only) |
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-03-17 01:33:48Z)**_

Perfect. I found the exact mechanism. Here's what you need to know:

## Disabling Hooks in Claude Code

There are **two ways** to disable hooks:

### 1. Global Disable Flag (Recommended for temporary bypass)

Add `"disableAllHooks": true` to any `settings.json` file:

```json
{
  "disableAllHooks": true,
  "hooks": { ... }
}
```

This disables all hooks without removing them from configuration. Changes take effect immediately.

### 2. Remove Specific Hook Entries

Delete individual hook entries from your settings file. Once removed, they won't run.

## Key Details

- **No individual hook toggle**: You cannot disable a single hook while keeping it in the config. Either delete it or use the global flag.

- **Settings file locations** where you can add the flag:
  - `~/.claude/settings.json` (all projects)
  - `.claude/settings.json` (single project)
  - `.claude/settings.local.json` (single project, local only)

- **Managed settings hierarchy**: If an administrator has set `disableAllHooks` in managed settings, user-level `disableAllHooks` cannot override it. The managed setting always takes precedence for enterprise security policies.

- **No CLI flag or slash command**: There is no command-line argument or slash command to bypass hooks.

**Your current setup** uses `~/.claude/settings.json` for user-level configuration, so adding `"disableAllHooks": true` there will temporarily disable all your hooks while keeping them configured.

Sources:
- [Hooks Configuration Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md)

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-17 01:33:54Z)**_

Yes. Add this to `/Users/gabrielamyot/.claude/settings.json` at the top level:

```json
"disableAllHooks": true
```

That turns off all hooks globally without removing them. Remove it (or set to `false`) to re-enable.

There's no per-hook toggle unfortunately, it's all or nothing.

_**User (2026-03-17 01:34:18Z)**_

can I comment hooks out in the json?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-17 01:34:26Z)**_

No. JSON doesn't support comments. Your options:

1. **`"disableAllHooks": true`** — kills all hooks, keeps config intact
2. **Delete the hook entry** — remove the specific block you don't want
3. **Change the matcher to something that'll never match** — e.g. rename `"Edit|Write"` to `"DISABLED_Edit|Write"` on the specific hook entry you want to skip

Option 3 is the closest thing to "commenting out" a single hook.
